purpose: Explains real economy resilience to QT by identifying the fiscal channel that mechanically supports aggregate corporate profits
components: Kalecki-Levy profit equation: aggregate corporate profits = investment + fiscal deficit + net exports + dividend consumption - household saving
With 6-7% GDP government deficits, the fiscal deficit term dominates, mechanically supporting aggregate profits
The accounting identity is not debatable — it describes an ex-post relationship
Buffer operates on aggregates — a leveraged entity facing margin calls is not protected by high aggregate profits
Implications: QT may never produce a recession as long as fiscal deficits remain large, but may still produce financial crises in leveraged channels
current_assessment: Provides the clearest explanation for why 45 months of QT have not produced a recession. The accounting identity is mechanically sound. Main limitation is that aggregate profit support does not prevent financial stress from propagating through leveraged channels. Strongest as a frame for past resilience; weakest as a prediction of indefinite resilience under acute financial stress.
limitations: Governs aggregates, not distribution — leveraged entities are not protected
Does not predict whether fiscal support prevents or merely delays a Minsky moment
Assumes continued large fiscal deficits — buffer disappears if fiscal policy tightens
Immunization framing overstates — the channel is a buffer, not a guarantee
Does not address compound tightening (simultaneous fiscal and monetary contraction, i.e., the 1937 scenario)
